description: This Prudential Standard applies to all ADIs except foreign ADIs and purchased payment facility providers, on both Level 1 and Level 2 bases.

Where an ADI is the holding company for a group, it must ensure requirements are met on a Level 2 basis. Where an ADI is a subsidiary of an authorised NOHC, the NOHC must ensure requirements are met on a Level 2 basis.

ADIs must apply this Standard to calculate RWA for credit exposures of overseas banking subsidiaries forming part of the Level 2 group, except where a prescribed New Zealand authority's equivalent prudential rules apply.
An ADI must hold Regulatory Capital commensurate with its credit risk exposure, applying specified risk weights and CCFs, and using ECAI ratings where appropriate.
An ADI must establish and implement effective internal policies, processes, systems and controls to ensure appropriate risk weights are assigned to its credit exposures.

If an ADI seeks to use LMI or eligible CRM techniques to reduce Regulatory Capital requirements, it must have effective policies, procedures and systems in place, including addressing residual risks such as legal, operational, liquidity and market risks.

An ADI must calculate RWA for on-balance sheet exposures by multiplying the current book value (including accrued interest or revaluations, net of provisions, write-offs and depreciation) by the relevant risk weight.
The RWA of a non-SFT off-balance sheet exposure must be calculated by first determining EAD per Attachment C or APS 180, then multiplying by the relevant risk weight.

For SFTs, an ADI must calculate RWA for counterparty exposure (EAD per Attachment G multiplied by relevant risk weight) and the capital requirement for credit or market risk in any securities lent or posted as collateral where that risk remains with the ADI.
Where multiple CRM techniques cover a single exposure, the ADI must divide the exposure into separately calculated portions and total the resulting RWA.
An ADI must not recognise additional CRM on exposures where the risk weight is mapped from an ECAI issue-specific credit rating that already incorporates CRM.
An ADI must not recognise CRM where there is a material positive correlation between the credit quality of the counterparty and the CRM technique used.

To obtain capital relief through CRM, all documentation must be legally binding and enforceable in all relevant jurisdictions, with sufficient legal review and periodic reviews to confirm ongoing enforceability.
Property exposures must be risk-weighted according to Attachment A requirements, except defaulted property exposures which must be risk-weighted under Attachment E.
A loan must meet all requirements in paragraphs 3 to 7 of Attachment A to be classified as a standard loan.

An ADI must have unequivocal enforcement rights over mortgaged property at all times, including right to possession and power of sale, and must reasonably consider it can realise the property's value within a reasonable timeframe.

An ADI's property exposure must be secured by a registered first mortgage, or a registered second mortgage meeting specified conditions including written consent of first mortgagee, restrictions on increasing the first mortgage amount, independent power of sale, and fair market value requirements on sale.

Prior to loan approval, an ADI must document, assess and verify the borrower's ability to meet repayment obligations, including assessment of higher interest rate impacts, all existing debt commitments, serviceability for P&I period on interest-only residential loans, and tenancy profile for cash-flow dependent commercial loans. Failure to make a positive determination requires classification as non-standard.

An ADI must appropriately value property offered as security per paragraph 11 of Attachment A, and except for ADC exposures, the property value must not depend materially on the borrower's performance.
All information required at loan origination and for monitoring must be accurately documented and readily accessible, including borrower repayment ability and property valuation information.

Property exposures originated prior to 1 January 2023 with no material change to loan terms and conditions after that date are exempt from the serviceability assessment requirements in paragraph 5 of Attachment A for standard loan classification.

LVR components must be prudently calculated per paragraphs 10 and 11 of Attachment A. The loan amount includes outstanding balance, accrued interest and fees, and any undrawn committed amount, gross of provisions and CRM. Multiple sequential loans secured by the same property must be aggregated; second mortgage loans must include all claims secured by both mortgages.

The value of a mortgaged property must be independently appraised using prudently conservative criteria consistent with APS 220, and must not exceed market value where determinable. The origination value must be maintained unless specific conditions for updating are met.
Where a residential property exposure is secured by both residential and commercial properties, an ADI must apply a 40 per cent haircut to the value of any commercial property when calculating LVR.

For reverse mortgages where a borrower has been guaranteed a minimum share in future sale proceeds, the LVR calculation must be adjusted by reducing the property value by the quarantined percentage share.

An ADI may use eligible CRM techniques to reduce the exposure amount of a property exposure, but the LVR band and applicable risk weight must be determined before the application of the relevant CRM technique.

Where finance is used for more than one purpose, the entire amount must be classified according to the predominant purpose. Where there is doubt about owner-occupied versus investment classification, the loan must be treated as other standard residential property.

An ADI may reduce its capital requirement through LMI only where the insurance provides cover for all losses up to at least 40 per cent of the higher of the original or outstanding loan amount, and is provided by an APRA-regulated insurer (Level 1) or overseas prudential regulator-regulated insurer (Level 2).
An ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 1 to standard residential property exposures based on loan classification (owner-occupied P&I or other standard), LMI status, and LVR band.

An ADI may apply a risk weight of 35 per cent to residential property exposures that satisfy the conditions for inclusion within the Australian Government's Home Guarantee Scheme and for which NHFIC has issued a guarantee certificate.
Interest-only loans with LVR greater than 80 per cent and an interest-only term greater than five years or of unspecified duration must be classified as non-standard loans.

An ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 2 to non-standard loans secured by residential property based on LVR. Reverse mortgages receive a 50 per cent risk weight at LVR ≤60% and 100 per cent above; all other non-standard loans receive 100 per cent.
A non-standard loan (due to failing paragraph 5 criteria) may be reclassified as a standard loan where the loan has been performing consecutively for the previous 36 months.
Where repayment of a commercial property exposure depends primarily on property cash flows, an ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 3 based on LVR and standard/non-standard classification.

Where repayment of a commercial property exposure does not depend primarily on property cash flows, an ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 4 based on counterparty type, LVR, and standard/non-standard classification.

To classify a commercial property exposure as not dependent on property cash flows, the borrower must have greater than $250 million in tangible assets, no single real estate asset may exceed 25 per cent of the portfolio by value, and tenant concentration limits must be met.

An ADI may apply a 100 per cent risk weight to ADC exposures secured by residential property only if the exposure meets standard loan criteria, total debt to qualifying development costs is less than 75 per cent, and pre-sales requirements are met for exposures above $5 million.
An ADI must apply a risk weight of 150 per cent to all ADC exposures that do not qualify for the 100 per cent risk weight under paragraph 29.

An ADI must risk-weight all non-property exposures in accordance with Attachment B, except for unsettled/failed transactions (Attachment D) and defaulted exposures (Attachment E). Where external credit ratings are used, Attachment F requirements must also be met.

An ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 5 to sovereign exposures based on external credit rating. Exposures to the Australian Government and RBA denominated and funded in Australian dollars may be risk-weighted at zero per cent.
An ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 6 to domestic PSE exposures that do not qualify as sovereign exposures, based on external credit rating.

An ADI must apply the risk weights in Tables 7 and 8 to bank exposures based on external credit rating and maturity. Unrated bank exposures must be risk-weighted as the higher of the standard unrated weight and the sovereign risk weight of the country of incorporation.
An ADI must not use external credit ratings that incorporate assumptions of implicit government support when assigning risk weights to bank exposures.

An ADI may apply the reduced risk weights in Table 9 to rated covered bond exposures only where the cover pool composition, overcollateralisation of at least 10 per cent, and ongoing semi-annual disclosure requirements are met.

Bank assets in a covered bond cover pool must not exceed 15 per cent of the cover pool. Residential property assets in the cover pool must have LVR ≤80% and commercial property assets must have LVR ≤60%.

An ADI must be able to demonstrate to APRA that cover pool information including value, geographical distribution, asset type, loan size, interest rate and currency risks is available on at least a semi-annual basis.
Unrated covered bonds or those not meeting paragraphs 15-16 requirements must be treated as bank exposures and risk-weighted using Table 7.
ADIs must apply risk weights from Table 10 to general corporate exposures with an external long-term credit rating.
ADIs must apply risk weights from Table 11 to short-term general corporate exposures with an issue-specific external credit rating.
Where a short-term issue-specific rating maps to grade 4, all unrated exposures to that counterparty must be risk-weighted at 150%.

An SME exposure is defined as one to a corporate counterparty with total consolidated annual revenue below $75 million; where revenue cannot be determined, exposures below $5 million may be treated as SME.
ADIs must apply risk weights from Table 12 to unrated SME exposures: 75% for SME retail and 85% for SME corporate.
To qualify as SME retail, the exposure must be a small business lending facility or commitment and the maximum aggregated exposure to the SME counterparty must be less than $1.5 million.

For unrated general corporate exposures not treated as SME, ADIs must apply 100% risk weight to all exposures, or 85% to investment grade and 110% to non-investment grade using an APRA-approved methodology.
ADIs must apply risk weights from Table 13 to unrated specialised lending exposures: 110% for project finance and 100% for object and commodities finance.
ADIs must apply risk weights from Table 14 to retail exposures: 75% for credit card exposures and 100% for other retail exposures.
Margin lending exposures secured by eligible financial collateral must be risk-weighted at 20%; those secured by other collateral must be risk-weighted at 100%.
Subordinated debt not required to be deducted from regulatory capital under APS 111 must be risk-weighted at 150%.
Equity exposures not deducted from regulatory capital must be risk-weighted at 250% if listed on a recognised exchange (or to banking/insurance subsidiaries at Level 1), and 400% if unlisted.

Where an ADI bears residual value risk in operating leases, it must apply 100% risk weight to the portion of aggregate residual value up to 10% of Tier 1 capital and 250% to the portion exceeding 10% of Tier 1 capital.

Credit exposures originated through a third party where the ADI does not conduct credit assessment, cannot administer workout/default processes, and has no direct recourse must be risk-weighted at 150%.

ADIs must apply risk weights from Table 16 to all other exposures not classified elsewhere, including 0% for cash and gold bullion, 20% for cash items in collection, and 100% for fixed assets and all other exposures.

For exposures originated after 1 January 2023, ADIs must apply a 1.5x multiplier to the applicable risk weight for unhedged retail or residential property exposures to individuals where the lending currency differs from the borrower's income currency.
An exposure may only be treated as hedged for currency mismatch purposes where the natural or financial hedge covers at least 90% of the loan repayment amount.
ADIs must multiply the committed but undrawn amount of an exposure by the relevant CCF from Table 17 and then risk-weight the resulting amount according to Attachments A, B and D.
Where an ADI has given a commitment to provide an off-balance sheet exposure, it may apply the lower of the CCFs applicable to the commitment and the off-balance sheet exposure.

ADIs must apply escalating risk weights from Table 18 to unsettled DvP transactions (excluding SFTs) based on the number of business days after the settlement date, applied to the positive current exposure amount.

ADIs must hold regulatory capital against non-DvP transactions where payment or delivery has been made without receipt, applying a 1250% risk weight from five business days after payment/delivery until extinction of the transaction.

For equity exposures, an ADI must apply risk weights based on the counterparty as per Attachment B, or where exposures are not material, apply a 100 per cent risk weight consistently across all such exposures.
An ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 19 to defaulted residential property exposures, calculated net of provisions and partial write-offs.
An ADI must apply the risk weights in Table 20 to defaulted non-residential property exposures, based on whether provisions are at least 20 per cent of the outstanding exposure amount.
An ADI may recognise eligible credit risk mitigation when calculating the exposure amount for defaulted exposures.
An ADI may only use solicited ECAI ratings to determine credit rating grades for risk-weighting, and must apply ratings consistently for each type of exposure.
An ADI must only use an ECAI rating that reflects the entire amount of credit risk exposure the ADI has with regard to all amounts owed to it.

An ADI must perform due diligence on external ratings and, where due diligence reflects higher risk than the external rating implies, must assign a risk weight at least one bucket higher; due diligence must not result in a lower risk weight.
An ADI must not use credit ratings for one entity within a corporate group to determine the risk weight for other entities within the same group.

Where a counterparty has multiple ECAI ratings mapping to different credit rating grades, an ADI must use the higher risk weight of the two lowest-risk-weight grades when three or more ratings exist, or the higher risk weight when two ratings exist.
An ADI must not use an ECAI rating denominated in one currency to derive the credit rating grade for an exposure denominated in another currency to the same counterparty.

An ADI must select either the simple or comprehensive approach for collateralised banking book exposures and apply it consistently; ADIs approved for the IRB approach to credit risk must not use the simple approach.
For trading book exposures where collateral has been received, an ADI must use the comprehensive approach.
An ADI acting as agent in an SFT that guarantees its principal's obligations must calculate a Regulatory Capital requirement as if it were the principal.

There must be a contract establishing the ADI's direct, explicit, irrevocable and unconditional recourse to received collateral; a common law right of set-off alone is insufficient for cash collateral.
An ADI must take all steps necessary to obtain and maintain an enforceable security interest in received collateral and have clear procedures for timely liquidation of collateral.
Where collateral is held by a custodian or third party, the ADI must take reasonable steps to ensure the collateral is segregated from other assets.

An ADI must ensure sufficient resources are devoted to the orderly operation of margin agreements and must have collateral risk management policies covering volatility, concentration, re-use, and surrender of rights.

Under the simple approach, received collateral must be pledged for at least the life of the exposure, marked-to-market and revalued at minimum every six months, and its release must be conditional on repayment of the exposure.

Under the simple approach, the risk weight on the collateral-secured portion of an exposure is subject to a floor of 20 per cent, except where specific conditions in paragraphs 18–20 apply; the unsecured portion is risk-weighted at the counterparty's applicable rate.

A zero per cent risk weight may be applied to collateralised transactions where the exposure and collateral are in the same currency and the collateral is either cash on deposit or sovereign securities eligible for zero per cent risk weight discounted by 20 per cent.
SFTs meeting all specified conditions may be risk-weighted at 10 per cent, or at zero per cent where the counterparty is a core market participant as defined in paragraph 20.
Under the comprehensive approach, an ADI must adjust EAD for collateralised transactions using the prescribed formula incorporating haircuts for collateral and foreign exchange.
Resecuritisation exposures as defined in APS 120 are not eligible financial collateral under either the simple or comprehensive approach.
For qualifying SFTs under paragraph 19, following a counterparty's failure to remargin, the time between the last mark-to-market and liquidation of collateral must be no more than four business days.

A maturity mismatch exists where the residual maturity of collateral lodgement is less than the maturity of the covered exposure; collateral with a maturity mismatch may only be recognised if the original maturity of lodgement is at least 12 months and the residual maturity is at least 3 months.

Where a maturity mismatch exists, an ADI must apply the prescribed adjustment formula to the collateral value, using the shortest possible effective maturity for collateral and the longest possible remaining time for the exposure.
An ADI must use the supervisory haircuts set out in Table 23 to determine haircuts for collateral and exposures across all eligible collateral types.
An ADI must apply a haircut of 8 per cent for currency risk where the exposure and collateral are denominated in different currencies, and zero per cent where they are in the same currency.
An ADI must apply the minimum holding periods and conditions in Table 24 to SFTs, other capital-market-driven transactions, and secured lending.
An ADI must apply a higher minimum holding period of 20 business days for the following quarter where a netting set has more than 5,000 trades at any point during a quarter.

An ADI must apply a minimum holding period of 20 business days for netting sets containing illiquid collateral or OTC derivatives that cannot be easily replaced, assessed under stressed market conditions.

Where an ADI has experienced more than two margin call disputes on a netting set over the previous two quarters lasting longer than the applicable minimum holding period, it must use a minimum holding period at least double the supervisory floor for that netting set for the subsequent two quarters.

An ADI must calculate the margining frequency adjustment factor for each transaction or netting set using the prescribed formula based on the minimum holding period and the number of business days between remarginings or revaluations.

For SFTs covered by an eligible bilateral netting agreement meeting Attachment H requirements, an ADI using the comprehensive approach must calculate EAD using the prescribed formula incorporating net and gross exposures, haircuts, and currency mismatches.
An ADI that chooses to net transactions must do so consistently and apply netting to all transactions in both the banking book and trading book covered by a netting agreement.

An ADI applying netting for Regulatory Capital purposes must have a netting policy addressing the requirements in this Prudential Standard, including systems and controls for monitoring and reporting netted transactions on a gross and net basis, and managing roll-off and termination risk.
An ADI must maintain adequate records to support the application of the netting agreement.

For Regulatory Capital purposes, an ADI may only net on-balance sheet loans and deposits, SFTs, and OTC derivative transactions with a single counterparty; netting across derivatives and SFTs is not permitted.

An ADI may only net internal transactions between the banking book and trading book if the netted transactions are marked-to-market daily and any collateralised instruments comply with eligible collateral criteria in Attachment G.

An ADI may only use net exposures of loans and deposits as the basis for RWA calculation where it has legal enforceability of the netting agreement, can identify covered loans and deposits at any time, and monitors and controls roll-off risks and net exposures.

An ADI must obtain a written and reasoned legal opinion confirming the enforceability of the netting agreement in all relevant jurisdictions, and maintain policies and procedures to keep legal enforceability under review.

An ADI must not recognise a netting agreement for Regulatory Capital purposes if there is any doubt as to its legal enforceability, including where a foreign regulator has indicated netting is not enforceable under its laws.

Where an exposure is covered by a guarantee from an eligible guarantor, an ADI may substitute the guarantor's risk weight for the counterparty's risk weight on the covered portion; the uncovered portion must be risk-weighted at the original counterparty rate.

To be recognised as eligible CRM, a guarantee must be a direct, explicitly documented, irrevocable, and unconditional claim on an eligible guarantor, covering all types of payments the borrower is expected to make.

An ADI must only recognise guarantees from sovereigns, banks, externally rated entities (for non-securitisation exposures), or entities with a credit rating grade of three or better (for securitisation exposures), where those entities have a lower risk weight than the counterparty.

A claim indirectly guaranteed by the Australian Government may be treated as directly guaranteed provided the indirect guarantee covers all credit risk elements, meets the requirements of paragraph 2 (except for directness and explicitness), and the ADI is satisfied the coverage is robust.
Letters of comfort do not qualify as eligible guarantees for CRM purposes.

Where a guarantee provides for a materiality threshold on payments below which no payment will be made in the event of loss, it is equivalent to a retained first loss position and must be deducted from Common Equity Tier 1 Capital in accordance with APS 111, capped at the capital required against the full exposure value.
Where there is partial coverage of an exposure by a guarantee and the covered and uncovered portions are of equal seniority, capital relief will be afforded on a proportional basis.
For securitisation exposures, eligible guarantors must have a credit rating grade of three or better and must have been externally rated two or better at the time the credit protection was provided.

Where a guarantee has a currency mismatch, an ADI must reduce the protected amount by applying an 8 per cent haircut based on a 10 business day holding period and daily marking-to-market, adjusted for actual revaluation frequency.
A guarantee with a maturity mismatch may only be recognised as eligible CRM if the original maturity is at least 12 months and the residual maturity is at least 3 months.

Where a single guarantor provides credit protection with different maturities to the same underlying exposure, an ADI must divide the exposure into separate covered portions for risk-weighting purposes.
Where there is a maturity mismatch on a guarantee, an ADI must apply the prescribed adjustment formula Pa = P × (t − 0.25) / (T − 0.25) to determine the adjusted guarantee value.

An ADI purchasing credit protection through an eligible credit derivative may substitute the risk weight of the counterparty with that of the credit protection seller for the covered portion; the uncovered portion retains the original counterparty risk weight.

Eligible credit derivatives for CRM purposes are limited to single-name credit-default swaps and certain total-rate-of-return swaps; total-rate-of-return swaps where net payments are recorded as income without offsetting asset value deterioration must not be recognised.

A credit derivative must meet all minimum requirements (paragraphs 5–7 of Attachment J) including being a direct claim, explicitly referenced, irrevocable, unconditional, specifying required credit events, not terminating before grace periods, and identifying credit event determination parties.

Where restructuring is excluded from a credit derivative contract but all other minimum requirements are met, an ADI may only recognise 60 per cent of the lesser of the credit protection amount and the underlying exposure amount.

An ADI must only recognise credit protection from eligible providers: sovereigns, banks, externally rated entities for non-securitisation exposures, and for securitisation exposures, entities rated grade three or better (and rated two or better at time of provision).

Where a credit derivative includes a materiality threshold below which no payment is made in the event of loss, it must be treated as a retained first loss position and deducted from Common Equity Tier 1 Capital in accordance with APS 111, capped at the capital required for the full exposure.

Where a credit derivative has a currency mismatch, an ADI must apply an 8 per cent haircut (based on a 10 business day holding period and daily marking-to-market) to the protected amount, adjusted for actual revaluation frequency.
A credit derivative with a maturity mismatch may only be recognised as eligible CRM if the original maturity is at least 12 months and the residual maturity is at least 3 months.
Where there is a maturity mismatch on a credit derivative, an ADI must apply the prescribed adjustment formula Pa = P × (t − 0.25) / (T − 0.25) to determine the adjusted credit protection value.

An ADI selling credit protection not covered by paragraphs 29–30 must undertake a written Regulatory Capital assessment prior to execution, notify APRA, and apply that treatment unless APRA determines otherwise.

An ADI that has sold credit protection using a credit derivative must assume that 100 per cent of the credit risk is purchased for capital adequacy purposes, irrespective of the range of specified credit events.

An ADI selling credit protection via a credit-default swap on a single reference entity must apply the risk weight applicable to that reference entity, with the exposure amount equal to the maximum possible payable under the contract.

For first-to-default basket credit derivatives, risk weights of basket assets must be aggregated up to a maximum of 1250 per cent and multiplied by the nominal protection amount to obtain the RWA; for second-to-default, the asset with the lowest risk-weighted amount may be excluded.
